title: Follow-up — Releng: user-session 2.1.0 release notes & code change
description:
The 14:01 deploy included user-session 2.0.4 → 2.1.0, a MINOR version bump (not patch). SRE, observability, and support all observe session invalidation every 2–3 minutes starting 14:02:14 UTC. All four domains converge on this being caused by the deploy.

Your task: (1) Retrieve the actual release notes for user-session 2.1.0. (2) Identify what behavior changed vs 2.0.4. (3) Cross-reference with SRE's observation: sessions are valid, then reported as expired, then new sessions are issued. Look for TTL changes, cache invalidation logic, session state handling, or token verification changes. (4) Explain why this would cause the 2–3 minute cycling pattern and why 92% of transactions failed but 8% succeeded.

This is the critical missing piece. Provide specific findings on what changed and how it explains the observed behavior.

title: Follow-up — Observability: Was 14:05 DB failover pre-scheduled?
description:
Your findings state: "Primary DB failover: 14:05:12 UTC initiated, 14:05:39 UTC completed. Routine weekly failover (scheduled Monday 14:05 slot)."

The session validation errors started at 14:02:14 UTC — 3 minutes BEFORE the failover. SRE asked whether the failover could have triggered cache evictions. You replied: "No evictions logged" and "cluster was healthy."

Your task: (1) Confirm the 14:05:12 failover was PRE-SCHEDULED (routine Monday maintenance) vs. triggered by alerts in response to the 14:02 spike. (2) If pre-scheduled, explicitly rule out any cascading effects on session cache hit rate during 14:02–14:05. (3) Confirm whether the DB failover touched the session Redis cluster at all, or was it only primary DB?

This will definitively rule out the DB failover as a contributing cause.

title: Follow-up — SRE: Session invalidation patterns & cache bypass
description:
Your logs show sessions cycling valid → 401 → valid → 401 every 2–3 minutes. Observability found that Redis cache-hit-rate dropped from 94.1% to 61.3% but the cluster was healthy (no evictions, no memory pressure).

Your task: (1) In your logs, can you identify patterns in which session invalidations triggered a cache miss vs. a cache hit? (2) Did newly-issued sessions (after re-auth) differ from the invalidated sessions in any way (TTL, encoding, key format)? (3) Among the 8% of checkouts that succeeded — can you identify any commonality? Did they use cached sessions, or did they avoid the validation path entirely?

This will help determine whether user-session 2.1.0 changed cache key generation, TTL handling, or validation logic.
